title: Algebraic Theory of Indefinite Theta Functions
speaker: Kenz Kallal
abstract: Jacobi's theta function Θ(q):=1+2q+2q4+2q9+⋯, and more generally the theta functions associated to positive-definite quadratic forms, have the property that they are modular forms of half-integral weight. The usual proof of this fact is completely analytic in nature, using the Poisson summation formula. However, Θ is also related to diffusion of heat on a uniform circle-shaped material: it is a restriction of the fundamental solution to the heat equation on a circle. By algebraically characterizing the heat equation as a specific flat connection on a certain bundle on a modular curve, we produce a completely algebraic technique for proving modularity of theta functions. More specifically, we produce a refinement of the algebraic theory of theta functions due to Moret-Bailly, Faltings--Chai, and Candelori. As a consequence of the algebraic nature of our theory and the fact that it applies to indefinite quadratic forms / non-ample line bundles (which the prior algebraic theory does not), we also generalize the Kudla--Millson analytic theory of theta functions for indefinite quadratic forms to the case of torsion coefficients. This is joint work in progress with Akshay Venkatesh.

proof_stages.label: Classical theta modularity and the indefinite obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a real quadratic lattice, recalls that the positive-definite theta series is modular by Poisson summation, and explains that in indefinite signature the naive series diverges and representation numbers may be infinite.

proof_stages.label: Kudla–Millson cohomological replacement
proof_stages.summary: Kudla–Millson theory replaces scalar representation numbers by degree-s cohomology classes for a congruence subgroup of the orthogonal group, producing a cohomology-valued modular form whose analytic proof uses differential forms and Poisson summation.

proof_stages.label: Integral algebraic reformulation over a coefficient ring
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the new algebraic version over a ring A with twice the discriminant inverted, producing A-valued cohomology classes and modular forms, so torsion classes can yield mod-p modular forms and possible p-adic variants.

proof_stages.label: Heat-equation substitute for Poisson summation
proof_stages.summary: A rank-one Jacobi-theta argument shows that uniqueness from quasi-periodicity plus the heat equation makes the modular transformation factor constant, identifying the heat equation as the intended replacement for Poisson summation.

proof_stages.label: Theta functions as algebraic families of line-bundle sections
proof_stages.summary: In the positive-definite case, higher-rank Jacobi theta functions are recast as sections of line bundles on powers of elliptic curves, and these line bundles and their spaces of sections are assembled into pushforward vector bundles over a modular curve.

proof_stages.label: Flat algebraic heat connection and positive-definite modularity
proof_stages.summary: A flat algebraic heat connection twisted by the Hodge bundle to the m/2 power makes theta horizontal; after choosing level to trivialize its finite Weil-representation monodromy, theta extends globally and evaluation at zero gives the desired modular form.

proof_stages.label: Indefinite upgrade via higher cohomology and equivariant evaluation
proof_stages.summary: For indefinite Q, the non-ample theta line bundle has cohomology in degree s, so toric-uniformization Fourier expansion principles and gamma-equivariant derived pushforwards are used to obtain an H^s(gamma,A)-valued modular form compatible with Kudla–Millson theory.

examples.name: Signature (2,1) Kudla–Millson region between planes
examples.note: In signature (2,1), a generic plane h and its translate gamma h bound a region whose lattice-point sum gives the modular form attached to gamma, making the Kudla–Millson cohomology class construction concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he draws the signature (2,1) cone and two planes, defines the region between h and gamma h, and forms the theta sum over lattice points in that region

examples.name: Torsion coefficient group Z plus Z/15
examples.note: A possible coefficient group such as Z ⊕ Z/15 illustrates that the cohomology-valued modular form can contain both characteristic-zero and mod-15 modular-form components.

examples.name: Rank-one Jacobi theta and the heat equation
examples.note: Working through the rank-one theta function shows that quasi-periodicity, the Gaussian factor under S, and the heat operator force the transformation scalar to be constant, visibly motivating the replacement of Poisson summation by an algebraic heat connection.
examples.engagement_reason: he runs the heat-equation proof for the rank-one Jacobi theta, writing the theta function, quasi-periodicity, gamma pullback, heat operator, and deriving that the multiplier is constant

passes.reason: The rank-one Jacobi theta is the minimal one-variable/base-case theta example used to demonstrate the heat-equation proof, not to disprove a claim.

examples.name: O(1,2) modular-curve torsion example
examples.note: For O(1,2) and a suitable gamma, the construction applied to torsion in the modular curve’s homology gives a mod-2 or mod-3 reduction of an Eisenstein/class-number series that becomes Jacobi theta, illustrating the arithmetic torsion output.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the construction to an O(1,2) choice giving a modular curve, discusses the torsion homology output, and identifies the resulting mod-2 or mod-3 series with Jacobi theta
